May or may not have spread to local lymph nodes Metastatic spreads to distant sites like other bones, liver, or brain (M1b); any grade

retrieved_docs.title: Primary Bone Cancer -- Staging -- Stage IA (G1 T1 M0)
retrieved_docs.content: Low grade, intra-compartmental, no metastasis

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Bone Markings -- Blood Supply and Lymphatics
retrieved_docs.content: Transcortical vessels traverse cortical bone, providing direct arteriovenous connections in the endosteum. The distribution and heterogeneity of perivascular cells in bone contribute to tissue homeostasis and repair. Different cell types associated with specific vessel subtypes, such as Nestin-expressing cells around type H vessels and LepR-expressing cells near type L vessels, exhibit multilineage potential. These cells contribute to bone marrow stroma, hematopoiesis, and adipogenesis, playing crucial roles in bone health and repair. Additionally, recent studies highlight the role of new adipogenic lineage cells called "marrow adipogenic lineage precursors" in maintaining marrow vasculature and modulating bone formation. [30]

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Bone Markings -- Structure and Function -- Sternum
retrieved_docs.content: The sternomanubrial angle (or sternal angle or angle of Louis) is the junction between the manubrium and sternal body (see Image . Sternum Anatomy). This angle is palpable as a slight elevation and serves as a landmark for locating the 2nd rib and the T4-T5 intervertebral disk. The sternal body (or gladiolus or gladiolus sterni) is the sternum's largest and central segment. The sternal margins provide attachment points for the ribs' costal cartilages. The jugular notch (suprasternal notch) is a U-shaped depression at the sternum's superior aspect, serving as a palpable landmark for anatomical reference and facilitating the identification of cervical structures. Clavicular notches on the manubrium's superior border articulate with the clavicles. [24] [25]

retrieved_docs.title: Primary Bone Cancer -- Staging -- Stage IB (T2 N0 M0 G1/GX, or T3 N0 M0 G1/GX)
retrieved_docs.content: >8cm in size (T2), with no lymph node or metastatic spread; low grade Cancer at more than 1 location in the same bone (T3), with no lymph node or metastatic spread; low grade

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Ethmoid Bone -- Embryology
retrieved_docs.content: The ethmoid bones develop primarily through endochondral ossification. The ethmoid bone starts to form at about 25 to 28 weeks of gestation. [2] Ethmoid sinuses are present at birth. Unlike all of the other sinuses, the ethmoid bone develops from the cartilaginous olfactory capsule or paleosinus while the other paranasal sinuses extend from the ethmoid into the membranous bone through epithelial diverticula extensions. Certain stages are necessary for the development of the paranasal sinus, which includes primary and secondary pneumatization patterns that call for differential growth of the cartilaginous olfactory capsule. These stages generate diverticular pouches that expand inside the boundaries of the capsule, which fabricate elaborate intracapsular airway spaces. [3] [1] Secondary pneumatization patterns occur within the paranasal recess, in which the diverticula exit the boundaries of the nasal capsule to become extracapsular and is left occupying space in adjacent structures, causing the paranasal recess to become a paranasal sinus. [4] Even though the size of the ethmoid bone becomes restrained, the sizes of the neosinus (which includes the frontal, maxillary, and sphenoidal) can fluctuate significantly because paranasal recesses are not uniform. The rate of development or differentiation in the extracapsular diverticula migration is not consistent, and when the extracapsular diverticula become extramural, they expand beyond the boundaries of the ethmoid bone and pneumatize nearby structures to generate different drainage pathways. [1]

retrieved_docs.title: Primary Bone Cancer -- Staging -- Stage III (T3 N0 M0 G2/G3)
retrieved_docs.content: Cancer at more than 1 location in the same bone, with no lymph node or metastatic spread; high grade

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Bone Markings -- Nerves
retrieved_docs.content: The periosteum exhibits similar quantities of A∂ and C fibers. In contrast, cortical bone and bone marrow contain more C than A∂ fibers. Sympathetic nerve fibers outnumber A∂ and C fibers in all compartments, indicating the prevalence of sympathetic innervation throughout bone tissue. [31]

retrieved_docs.title: Primary Bone Cancer -- Staging -- Stage IB (G1 T2 M0)
retrieved_docs.content: Low grade, extra compartmental, no metastasis

retrieved_docs.title: Primary Bone Cancer -- Staging -- Stage IA (T1 N0 M0 G1/GX)
retrieved_docs.content: ≤8cm in size, with no lymph node or metastatic spread Low grade

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Bone Markings -- Nerves
retrieved_docs.content: Steverink et al's study reveals that nerve fiber distribution within the bone and bone-related compartments contain innervation density and fiber type variations. The periosteum harbors greater nerve fiber density, particularly A∂, C, and sympathetic nerve fibers, than cortical bone and bone marrow. Periosteal and bone marrow sensory fibers form large bundles, while cortical bone predominantly contains single fibers confined to Haversian canals. Sympathetic fibers are colocalized with blood vessels in all compartments, suggesting a role in vascular regulation. Confocal imaging of periosteal whole-mount samples illustrates a branched network of nerve fibers, indicating extensive innervation throughout the periosteum, particularly around blood vessels.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Bone Markings -- Structure and Function -- Common Bone Markings
retrieved_docs.content: Notch: A bony depression that often, but not always, stabilizes an adjacent articulating bone. The articulating bone slides into and out of the notch, which guides the joint's range of motion. Examples include the ulna's trochlear and radial notches and the suprasternal and mandibular notches.

retrieved_docs.title: Lisfranc Dislocation -- Staging
retrieved_docs.content: The most recent and widely accepted classification of these injuries is Myerson's classification, which is derived from the previous two. According to this Myerson, Lisfranc injuries must be categorized as follows: Type A: total lateral or dorsoplantar incongruity, with all 5 metatarsal bases displaced in the same direction Type B: isolated incongruity B1: medial M1 displacement B2: lateral or at least 1 of the lateral 4 metatarsals is displaced Type C C1: partial divergent C2: total divergent Type D D1: distance between C1 and M2 is 2 mm or less (does not require operative fixation) D2: distance between C1 and M2 is greater than 2 mm (requires surgery) D2L: purely ligamentous D2B: with bone avulsion

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Bone Markings -- Blood Supply and Lymphatics
retrieved_docs.content: The bone capillary network fills the marrow cavity, predominantly comprising dense, fenestrated, and branched sinusoidal vessels. Linear columnar vessels predominate in the metaphysis and endosteum, interconnected by loops or arches. These vessels drain into a central vein in long bone diaphyses, with smaller veins branching off to periosteal veins. Type H vessels, expressing markers like endomucin and CD31, exhibit higher oxygen levels and blood velocity due to direct arteriolar connections, influencing tissue microenvironments. Various cell types surrounding blood vessels, like pericytes and stromal cells, show distinct characteristics depending on the vessel type and share similarities with mesenchymal progenitor cells, suggesting a role in tissue repair. These cells contribute to bone lineage during development and adulthood, supporting diverse cellular environments within bone tissue.

retrieved_docs.content: Any grade, any location, regional, or distant metastatic spread

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 2556
retrieved_docs.content: Primary malignant neoplasm of the nasal bone and primary malignant neoplasm of the thymus are both examples of primary malignant neoplastic proliferations, representing pathological processes in which cancerous cells originate and grow aggressively at their respective anatomical sites. As primary malignant neoplastic proliferations, each condition reflects a direct, localized malignant transformation of tissue—one affecting the nasal bone and the other affecting the thymus. Recognizing that both entities share the same fundamental nature as pathological processes of primary malignant neoplastic growth aids clinicians in understanding their aggressive behavior, guiding diagnostic evaluation, and informing treatment strategies tailored to the specific organ involved.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 2 -- Community 1224
retrieved_docs.content: Metastatic malignant neoplasm to ethmoid bone, metastatic malignant neoplasm to frontal bone, and metastatic malignant neoplasm to temporal bone (diagnosis) are each classified as malignant neoplasms of the ethmoid bone, frontal bone, and temporal bone respectively. These site‑specific malignant bone tumors are encompassed within the broader diagnostic category “cancer of bone and connective tissue,” which is mapped to each of the three bone‑specific malignant neoplasms. Consequently, the three metastatic bone cancers are recognized as particular manifestations of bone and connective tissue cancer, highlighting their shared pathology while preserving their distinct anatomical locations.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 3 -- Community 2437
retrieved_docs.content: Metastatic Lesion and Secondary Lesion are both recognized as diagnostic findings of the disease سرطانة العظم. In patients with سرطانة العظم, the identification of metastatic lesions indicates that malignant cells have spread to bone from another primary site, while secondary lesions represent additional tumor deposits that develop as a consequence of the primary bone malignancy. Recognizing these lesions is essential for accurate diagnosis, staging, and the formulation of appropriate treatment strategies for سرطانة العظم.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 2 -- Community 362
retrieved_docs.content: Other bone disease and musculoskeletal deformities are associated with bone resorption, indicating that these conditions often involve increased breakdown of bone tissue, which contributes to skeletal weakening and structural abnormalities.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 15 -- Level 1 -- Community 248
retrieved_docs.content: includes Primary malignant neoplasm of bone of face, which is mapped_to cancer of bone and connective tissue. Cancer of bone and connective tissue is a broad category that encompasses malignant neoplasms of individual cranial bones—malignant neoplasm of ethmoid bone, frontal bone, occipital bone, parietal bone, and temporal bone—as well as the Arabic term ورم العظام (bone tumor). Each site‑specific malignant neoplasm (e.g., malignant neoplasm of occipital bone, parietal bone, temporal bone) is mapped to cancer of bone and connective tissue, and metastatic forms (Metastatic malignant neoplasm to ethmoid bone, frontal bone, occipital bone, parietal bone, temporal bone) are classified as malignant neoplasms of the respective bones. Additionally, Metastasis to head and neck lymph node is an inverse_isa of سرطان الرأس والعنق, highlighting its role as a specific manifestation of head‑and‑neck cancer. Together, these interrelated entities illustrate that malignant bone tumors of the skull

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 27 -- Level 1 -- Community 211
retrieved_docs.content: (spread to bone), indicating that metastatic dissemination to bone is a recognized pathway for these lesions. Collectively, these interrelated concepts illustrate a comprehensive framework in which lymph‑node tumors, lymph‑node cancer, and metastatic spread to lymph nodes are nested within broader classifications of hematopoietic, lymphatic, and unspecified malignant neoplasms, while parallel relationships connect bone‑related malignancies, their uncertain benign‑malignant status, and patterns of bone metastasis. This integrated hierarchy supports a nuanced understanding of how specific tumor entities relate to larger disease categories, anatomic sites, and metastatic pathways, which is essential for accurate diagnosis, staging, and therapeutic planning.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 2 -- Community 1151
retrieved_docs.content: Metastatic malignant neoplasm to ethmoid bone, metastatic malignant neoplasm to occipital bone, and metastatic malignant neoplasm to temporal bone (diagnosis) are each classified as malignant neoplasm of ethmoid bone, malignant neoplasm of occipital bone, and malignant neoplasm of temporal bone respectively. These site‑specific malignant bone tumors are encompassed within the broader diagnostic category of cancer of bone and connective tissue, to which each of the three bone‑specific neoplasms is mapped. Recognizing that metastatic lesions to the ethmoid, occipital, and temporal bones represent distinct manifestations of bone and connective tissue cancer aids clinicians in accurate coding, staging, and treatment planning for patients with skeletal metastases.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 23 -- Level 3 -- Community 2513
retrieved_docs.content: Malignant Cellular Infiltrate and Metastatic Lesion are both recognized as pathological findings of سرطانة العظم (bone cancer). The presence of a malignant cellular infiltrate indicates that cancerous cells have invaded the bone tissue, while a metastatic lesion reflects the spread of tumor cells to bone from another primary site. Identifying either of these findings on imaging or histopathology supports the diagnosis of سرطانة العظم and guides clinicians in staging the disease, planning appropriate treatment, and monitoring for disease progression.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 1 -- Community 144
retrieved_docs.content: and serves as a broader class for primary malignant neoplasm of bone of the face, which maps to cancer of bone and connective tissue, a higher‑level relationship that includes the generic Arabic term ورم العظام (bone tumor) and the specific cranial bone malignancies listed above. Collectively, these relationships illustrate that metastatic lesions to skin, head‑and‑neck lymph nodes, and cranial bones are manifestations of broader bone‑and‑connective‑tissue cancers, share the metastatic tumor morphology الورم النقيلي, and are organized across multilingual taxonomies (Arabic, Dutch, Spanish, Czech) into coherent anatomical and pathological hierarchies that support precise diagnosis, staging, and treatment planning.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 26 -- Level 2 -- Community 1119
retrieved_docs.content: Metastatic malignant neoplasm to frontal bone is classified as a malignant neoplasm of frontal bone, and Metastatic malignant neoplasm to parietal bone is classified as a malignant neoplasm of parietal bone. Both of these site‑specific malignant neoplasms are encompassed by the broader diagnostic category cancer of bone and connective tissue, which is also referred to in Arabic as ورم العظام. By mapping the malignant neoplasms of the frontal and parietal bones to cancer of bone and connective tissue, clinicians can recognize these metastatic lesions as part of the overall spectrum of bone‑related cancers, facilitating accurate classification, staging, and therapeutic decision‑making.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 2 -- Community 1214
retrieved_docs.content: Metastatic Lesion and Secondary Lesion are clinical findings that can be observed in patients with سرطانة العظم (bone cancer). Both entities may present as Metastatic malignant neoplasm to supraclavicular lymph nodes, indicating that the primary bone malignancy has spread to the supraclavicular lymphatic region. Recognizing Metastatic Lesion and Secondary Lesion as findings of bone cancer and of metastatic malignant neoplasm to supraclavicular lymph nodes is important for accurate staging, prognosis assessment, and the selection of appropriate therapeutic strategies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2190
retrieved_docs.content: Metastatic malignant neoplasm to the parietal bone is classified as a malignant neoplasm of the parietal bone, indicating that cancer that has spread to this skull bone falls within the broader category of primary malignant bone tumors of the parietal region. Recognizing this relationship aids clinicians in accurately diagnosing, staging, and formulating appropriate therapeutic strategies for patients with metastatic involvement of the parietal bone.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 3 -- Community 2655
retrieved_docs.content: Malignant Cellular Infiltrate and Secondary Lesion are both recognized as diagnostic findings of سرطانة العظم, indicating the presence of bone cancer. The identification of a malignant cellular infiltrate reflects the invasion of cancerous cells into bone tissue, while a secondary lesion denotes additional disease sites that arise as the primary bone tumor progresses. Recognizing these findings together aids clinicians in confirming the diagnosis of سرطانة العظم, assessing disease extent, and guiding appropriate therapeutic planning.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2187
retrieved_docs.content: Cancer of bone and connective tissue is a malignant condition that involves the skeletal system and its supporting connective tissues. It has a broader relationship with ورم العظام, indicating that bone tumors (ورم العظام) are encompassed within this wider category of cancer. Recognizing that ورم العظام represents a specific manifestation of cancer of bone and connective tissue aids clinicians in accurate diagnosis, appropriate classification, and targeted therapeutic planning.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 1 -- Community 215
retrieved_docs.content: Metastatic malignant neoplasm to ethmoid bone is classified as malignant neoplasm of ethmoid bone, and metastatic malignant neoplasm to frontal bone, occipital bone, parietal bone, and temporal bone are each classified as malignant neoplasm of frontal bone, malignant neoplasm of occipital bone, malignant neoplasm of parietal bone, and malignant neoplasm of temporal bone (diagnosis) respectively. These site‑specific bone malignancies are all encompassed by the broader category cancer of bone and connective tissue, which also includes the Arabic terms ورم العظام (bone tumor) and ورم خبيث في الفك العلوي (malignant tumor of the upper jaw). Neoplasm of bone of face serves as a general class for Primary malignant neoplasm of bone of face, and Primary malignant neoplasm of bone of face is mapped to cancer of bone and connective tissue, linking facial bone cancers to the same overarching disease group. The Arabic designation سرطان العظم (bone cancer) is used to classify ورم خبيث في الفك العلوي, further illustrating that malignant tumors of the upper jaw are regarded as a form of bone cancer. Collectively, these relationships demonstrate that metastatic and primary malignant bone tumors of the skull and facial bones are organized under the unified concept of cancer of bone and connective tissue, providing a comprehensive framework for diagnosis, classification, and patient management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 33 -- Level 3 -- Community 2564
retrieved_docs.content: Cancer of bone and connective tissue is mapped to ورم خبيث في الفك العلوي, indicating that this malignant condition involves the upper jaw and reflects a neoplastic process affecting both bone and connective tissue structures in that region.
